title: Product Liability (CPA 1987)

statuteRef.label: Actions in respect of defective products

questions.label: Date the product was supplied to the person who suffered damage
questions.helpText: The 10-year longstop under Sch.1 para.1 of the Consumer Protection Act 1987 runs from the date the product was last supplied by a person to whom s.2(2) applies. This is typically the date the specific product unit entered the supply chain from the producer.

questions.label: Date damage first occurred
questions.helpText: The date on which the claimant first suffered damage caused by the defective product.

questions.label: Do you know the claimant's date of knowledge of the damage?
questions.helpText: Under s.14(1A) Limitation Act 1980, the date of knowledge for product liability claims is when the claimant first knew: (a) the damage was significant, (b) it was caused wholly or partly by the product defect, (c) the defendant's identity.

questions.label: Date of knowledge
questions.helpText: The earliest date the claimant knew or ought reasonably to have known of the material facts giving rise to the claim.

questions.label: Was the claimant under a disability at the date of accrual?
questions.helpText: Under the Limitation Act 1980, a person is under a disability if they are a minor (under 18) or lack capacity within the meaning of the Mental Capacity Act 2005.

questions.label: Date disability ceased
questions.helpText: The date the claimant turned 18, or the date capacity was regained.

notes: Product liability claims under the CPA 1987 are strict liability — no need to prove negligence, only that the product was defective.
The 10-year longstop is absolute and cannot be extended by any modifier, including disability.
The court has NO discretion under s.33 to disapply the time limit for CPA claims (unlike personal injury claims).
A parallel claim in negligence may also be available with different limitation rules.
